## Statistics and records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brisbane_Broncos&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Statistics and records")]

Further information: [Brisbane Broncos Records](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos_Records "Brisbane Broncos Records")

In their thirty-nine completed seasons, the club has made a total of nine Grand Finals, winning seven and losing two; once to the North Queensland Cowboys in 2015, and once to the Penrith Panthers in 2023.[[83]](#cite_note-83) They are one of only two clubs to have won the [World Club Challenge](/wiki/World_Club_Challenge "World Club Challenge") twice, and were the first club to do so on British soil. They also won the now defunct [Panasonic Cup](/wiki/Amco_Cup "Amco Cup") in [1989](/wiki/1989_Panasonic_Cup_(rugby_league) "1989 Panasonic Cup (rugby league)").[[47]](#cite_note-fabulous-47)

Former team captain Darren Lockyer holds the record for the most First Grade games for the club. Lockyer also held the record for the most points scored for the club, tallying 1,171 since his debut in 1995, before Corey Parker overtook him in August 2015 with 1,222 career points for Brisbane Broncos. Darren Lockyer also holds the club record of 272 points in a season, having achieved this in 1998. [Darren Lockyer](/wiki/Darren_Lockyer "Darren Lockyer") was named [Fullback](/wiki/Fullback_(rugby_league) "Fullback (rugby league)") in Queensland Rugby League's Team of the Century in 1998–2007 and he won 4 grand finals with the Brisbane Broncos (including a [Clive Churchill Medal](/wiki/Clive_Churchill_Medal "Clive Churchill Medal")) and a World Cup title with Australia. He also won the Golden Boot Award for world's best player in this position before switching to Five-eighth.

Steve Renouf also shares the club record for the most tries in a season with [Darren Smith](/wiki/Darren_Smith_(rugby_league) "Darren Smith (rugby league)") at 23. After over a decade after [Steve Renouf](/wiki/Steve_Renouf "Steve Renouf")'s move from the Brisbane Broncos to Wigan Warriors, he was still the club's all-time try scorer with a 142 career tries. Five times, he scored 4 tries in a single match from 1991 to 1998 and was known as one of the greatest centres the game has ever seen. In 2008, Steve Renouf was named centre in the Indigenous Team of the Century.

[Lote Tuqiri](/wiki/Lote_Tuqiri_(rugby,_born_1979) "Lote Tuqiri (rugby, born 1979)")'s tally of 26 points from three tries and seven goals in a single match against the [Northern Eagles](/wiki/Northern_Eagles "Northern Eagles") remains the club record for most individual points in a game.[[84]](#cite_note-broncsrecords-84) He also won the 1999 Brisbane Broncos season's rookie of the year award.

[Corey Parker](/wiki/Corey_Parker_(rugby_league) "Corey Parker (rugby league)") holds the record for most goals in a game kicking ten in a round one clash of 2008, breaking the previous mark of nine kicked by Lockyer in 1998 and matched by [Michael De Vere](/wiki/Michael_De_Vere "Michael De Vere") in 2001. He also scored a try on debut during 2001. Parker converted ten from ten goals in the Broncos 48–12 win over the [Penrith Panthers](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers") in which Parker scored 24 points placing him in equal second place on the most points in a match tally. In 2013, Corey Parker was named Dally M Lock of the Year and in 2009 received the Broncos' Paul Morgan Award. Corey Parker also received the Paul Morgan Award yet again in 2013 and 2015.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86) Six players have scored four tries in a match for the Brisbane Broncos including [Steve Renouf](/wiki/Steve_Renouf "Steve Renouf") (5 times), [Wendell Sailor](/wiki/Wendell_Sailor "Wendell Sailor"), [Karmichael Hunt](/wiki/Karmichael_Hunt "Karmichael Hunt"), [Justin Hodges](/wiki/Justin_Hodges "Justin Hodges"), [Denan Kemp](/wiki/Denan_Kemp "Denan Kemp") and former Australian Wallabies player, [Israel Folau](/wiki/Israel_Folau "Israel Folau").[[84]](#cite_note-broncsrecords-84) [Wendell Sailor](/wiki/Wendell_Sailor "Wendell Sailor") has held the record for the most tries scored in a finals match (four tries against St. George Illawarra Dragons, Semi-final 2001, Brisbane won 44–28).

The most field goals in a match however hasn't been past one field goal which has been accomplished several times.

The club's biggest winning margin is 65 points, achieved in 2007 in a 71–6 victory over the [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights").[[87]](#cite_note-87) Their heaviest defeat is a 59–0 loss by the Sydney Roosters on 4 June 2020 .[[88]](#cite_note-88) The club's highest winning margin in a Grand Final is by 26 points (38–12, against [Canterbury Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs"), 1998).

In the 2017 NRL season, the Brisbane Broncos' highest try-scorer was former Gold Coast Titans centre James Roberts with 15 tries. The highest points-scorer was Jordan Kahu with 67 goals and 8 tries. The most metres run in the season was by Tautau Moga with 3410 metres.

## Club Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brisbane_Broncos&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Club Rivalries")]

### North Queensland Cowboys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brisbane_Broncos&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: North Queensland Cowboys")]

Main article: [Queensland derby](/wiki/Queensland_derby "Queensland derby")

Brisbane's biggest rivals are the [North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys "North Queensland Cowboys"). The two clubs had a long-standing 'sibling rivalry' with North Queensland characterized as the underdog. Brisbane initially were dominant in the fixture with Brisbane going undefeated against North Queensland for the first nine years until North Queensland recorded their first ever victory in the 2004 elimination final defeating Brisbane 10–0.

The rivalry reached new heights after North Queensland won their first premiership, beating Brisbane at the 2015 Grand Final at [Stadium Australia](/wiki/Stadium_Australia "Stadium Australia"). [Considered one of the greatest grand finals of all time](/wiki/2015_NRL_Grand_Final "2015 NRL Grand Final"), the win established North Queensland as competitive equals. Between 2015 and 2017 a staggering three of five games played between the two clubs led to a Golden Point finish. In the final round of the [2020 NRL season](/wiki/2020_NRL_season "2020 NRL season"), North Queensland condemned Brisbane to their first ever [Wooden Spoon](/wiki/Wooden_Spoon_(award) "Wooden Spoon (award)"). Brisbane needed to win the match to overtake [Canterbury-Bankstown](/wiki/Canterbury_Bulldogs "Canterbury Bulldogs") but lost the match.

It is regarded as one of the greatest modern sports rivalries earning the nickname of the "[Queensland derby](/wiki/Queensland_derby "Queensland derby")".[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[24]](#cite_note-nrl.com-24)

### Gold Coast Titans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brisbane_Broncos&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Gold Coast Titans")]

Main article: [South East Queensland Derby](/wiki/South_East_Queensland_Derby "South East Queensland Derby")

The match between Brisbane and the Gold Coast is known as the South Queensland Derby. The media and supporters of both club's have described this fixture as "Big Brother against Little Brother". Brisbane are the more successful team in the fixture with the Gold Coast only defeating Brisbane eleven times since 2007. The two club's have only met in two finals matches, the first being in 2009 when Brisbane won 40–32. The other time being in 2016 when the Gold Coast reached the finals by default due to Parramatta's salary cap points deduction. Brisbane won the match 44–28.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

The [Gold Coast](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Titans "Gold Coast Titans") are geographically the closest club to Brisbane. Brisbane previously enjoyed derbies with the [Gold Coast Chargers](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Chargers "Gold Coast Chargers"), until the Chargers exited the competition at the end of 1998 season, returning as the [Gold Coast Titans](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Titans "Gold Coast Titans") in 2007 and before that with the Gold Coast Seagulls and Gold Coast-Tweed Giants. In the [2021 NRL season](/wiki/2021_NRL_season "2021 NRL season"), Brisbane recorded the biggest ever comeback in their history by defeating the Gold Coast 36–28 after being down 22–0.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

### Melbourne Storm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brisbane_Broncos&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Melbourne Storm")]

Main article: [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm")

Since the [2006 NRL Grand Final](/wiki/2006_NRL_Grand_Final "2006 NRL Grand Final") where Brisbane edged out Melbourne 15–8, a rivalry still continues today between the two clubs. The two sides have played each other in multiple finals matches. Adding to this, both Brisbane and Melbourne players usually feature heavily in Queensland's annual State of Origin side. Since the 2006 decider, Brisbane have played Melbourne on thirty-four occasions and only won five times. In round 27 of the [2023 NRL season](/wiki/2023_NRL_season "2023 NRL season"), the two sides met in the final round of the regular campaign. Brisbane and Melbourne both rested regular players as each team had already qualified for the finals. Melbourne won 32–22 and by the end of the round, Brisbane missed out on their first [Minor Premiership](/wiki/Minor_Premiership "Minor Premiership") win since [2000](/wiki/2000_NRL_season "2000 NRL season").[[38]](#cite_note-espn.co.uk-38)[[94]](#cite_note-94)
The two sides would meet the following week in the 2023 qualifying final with Brisbane defeating Melbourne 26–0 to book a place in the preliminary final. It was the first time Brisbane had beaten Melbourne at Lang Park since 2009 and the first time that they had defeated Melbourne in 14 attempts.[[95]](#cite_note-95)
The rivalry culminated in the 2025 NRL Grand Final, where Brisbane produced a composed and decisive performance to defeat Melbourne and claim their first premiership since 2006. The result was seen as a defining moment in the Broncos’ modern history, reaffirming the club’s status as a dominant force in the competition and ending a prolonged period of Melbourne ascendancy in their finals encounters.

### Dolphins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brisbane_Broncos&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Dolphins")]

Main article: [Battle for Brisbane (rugby league)](/wiki/Battle_for_Brisbane_(rugby_league) "Battle for Brisbane (rugby league)")

"The Battle for Brisbane" is a new rivalry which began with the Dolphins' admission as the second Brisbane club in 2023. This marked the first time that Brisbane has had two professional rugby league teams since the demise of the [South Queensland Crushers](/wiki/South_Queensland_Crushers "South Queensland Crushers") in 1997. In round 4 of the [2023 NRL season](/wiki/2023_NRL_season "2023 NRL season"), the two sides played against each other for the first time. In a match which saw both sides take the lead, Brisbane would go on to win 18–12 in front of 51,047 fans. This was the biggest crowd at [Lang Park](/wiki/Suncorp_Stadium "Suncorp Stadium") for a regular season game in its history.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

## Club affiliations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brisbane_Broncos&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Club affiliations")]

The Brisbane Broncos have three split feeder clubs from the Queensland Cup: [Northern Suburbs Devils](/wiki/Norths_Devils "Norths Devils"), [Souths Logan Magpies](/wiki/Souths_Logan_Magpies "Souths Logan Magpies") and [Wynnum-Manly Seagulls](/wiki/Wynnum_Manly_Seagulls "Wynnum Manly Seagulls"). Former feeder clubs of the Broncos are the now-defunct [Aspley Broncos](/wiki/Aspley_Broncos "Aspley Broncos") and [Toowoomba Clydesdales](/wiki/Toowoomba_Clydesdales "Toowoomba Clydesdales"), active clubs [Central Queensland Capras](/wiki/Central_Queensland_Capras "Central Queensland Capras"), who switched to [The Dolphins](/wiki/Dolphins_(NRL) "Dolphins (NRL)"), [Ipswich Jets](/wiki/Ipswich_Jets "Ipswich Jets") who switched to [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights"), [Redcliffe Dolphins](/wiki/Redcliffe_Dolphins "Redcliffe Dolphins") who switched to the [Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors"), and [Burleigh Bears](/wiki/Burleigh_Bears "Burleigh Bears") who switched to the [Gold Coast Titans](/wiki/Gold_Coast_Titans "Gold Coast Titans").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Women's team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brisbane_Broncos&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Women's team")]

Main article: [Brisbane Broncos Women](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos_Women "Brisbane Broncos Women")

In 2017, the Brisbane Broncos launched a bid to enter a team in the inaugural [NRL Women's Premiership](/wiki/NRL_Women%27s_Premiership "NRL Women's Premiership") in [2018](/wiki/2018_NRL_Women%27s_season "2018 NRL Women's season"). On 27 March 2018, the club won a license to participate in the inaugural NRL Women's season, on the back of a strong bid which included the NRL's desire for a geographical spread.[[97]](#cite_note-won-license-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) [Paul Dyer](/w/index.php?title=Paul_Dyer_(rugby_league)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Paul Dyer (rugby league) (page does not exist)") was named as the coach of the women's side,[[99]](#cite_note-99) but stepped down after the inaugural season to concentrate on his role as game development manager. Kelvin Wright was named his replacement in May 2019.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

In June 2018, [Ali Brigginshaw](/wiki/Ali_Brigginshaw "Ali Brigginshaw"), [Brittany Breayley](/wiki/Brittany_Breayley "Brittany Breayley"), [Heather Ballinger](/wiki/Heather_Ballinger "Heather Ballinger"), [Teuila Fotu-Moala](/wiki/Teuila_Fotu-Moala "Teuila Fotu-Moala") and [Caitlyn Moran](/wiki/Caitlin_Moran_(rugby_league) "Caitlin Moran (rugby league)") were unveiled as the club's first five signings.[[101]](#cite_note-101) Tain Drinkwater was also appointed the CEO of the team.[[102]](#cite_note-102)

The club won the inaugural NRL Women's Premiership title by defeating the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters_(NRLW) "Sydney Roosters (NRLW)") by 34–12 in the [2018 NRL Women's Premiership Grand Final](/wiki/2018_NRL_Women%27s_Premiership_Grand_Final "2018 NRL Women's Premiership Grand Final").[[103]](#cite_note-NRLWpremiers-103)

The Brisbane Broncos went back-to-back in 2019, with a dominant 30-6 win over the St George Illawarra Dragons.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

In 2020, a year the competition was impacted by COVID 19, the Broncos made it back-to-back titles, again defeating the Sydney Roosters in the decider, winning their third title, 20 points to 10.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

In 2025, the Broncos defeated the Sydney Roosters 22 points to 18 in the NRLW Grand Final at Accor Stadium, to win their fourth title.[[106]](#cite_note-106)
